the structure of DNA. DNA is a molecule composed of a backbone of deoxyribose sugar and phosphate groups, with two “legs” of alternating bases of adenine (A), thymine (T), cytosine (C), and guanine (G). The bases are always paired in the correct way, A with T and C with G. The molecules are arranged in a double helix, with the “legs” twisted around each other to make a spiral staircase. The bases are held in place by strong chemical bonds. The A-T and C-G pairs are held together by hydrogen bonds, and these bonds are the weakest. When a single strand of DNA is heated, the hydrogen bonds between the bases break, and the two strands separate. This process is called denaturation, and it demonstrates that the two strands of DNA are held together by weak bonds.
